name: flowcraft

commit.message: lockfile
bump

docs: add loop controller error handling and conditional edges documentation

- Add Error Handling section documenting validation when loop controller
lacks continue edge
- Document conditional edges from loop controllers for early loop exit
- Add runtime validation errors section in API errors reference with
loop controller example
chore: minor dependencies

fix(runtime): evaluate conditional edges from loop controllers

Add special handling for loop-controller nodes in determineNextNodes().
Instead of only matching edges by action ('continue'/'break'), evaluate
all outgoing edges with conditions first. This allows conditional edges
from loop controllers to trigger early exit when their conditions are
met.

Add test to verify conditional edges from loop controllers are evaluated
on each iteration.

fix(runtime): throw error when loop controller lacks continue edge

Add fail-fast validation in GraphTraverser.filterNodesInLoops() to throw
a descriptive FlowcraftError when a loop controller is missing the
required continue edge. This prevents silent failures when loops are
improperly wired.

Add unit test to verify the error is thrown with appropriate message.

fix(runtime): refactor loop handling and fix sibling execution #8

- Refactor loop detection to reset all nodes inside a loop for re-execution
- Fix loop siblings not executing by removing dead code in determineNextNode
- Update loop definition pattern: set loop start/end, wire edges inside
- Update tests and docs to reflect new loop handling
- Apply lint fixes

* fix(core/traverser & loop-controller & tests): refactor `loop` usage, fix traverser loop handling

- instead of relying on `any` join strategy for handling loop execution, now it detects all nodes inside a loop and resets all the nodes in that loop as uncompleted to re-execute the loop when a loop is about to start. This can ensure a loop with >= 3 nodes can run and make execution more stable (`any` can break in-loop logic).
- use more elegant and intuitive method to define loops: set loop start and end, wire edges **inside the loop**, then wire incoming and breaking nodes directly to the loop instead of nodes inside the loop. This reduces rewiring overhead and is the similar pattern used by popular workflow products.
- update tests to reflect new loop usage.
- add one test that tests whether a loop with >= 3 nodes can run.
- update docs to reflect new loop usage.
- fix small flaws in docs.
- examined `@flowcraft/compiler` logic, found its tests contains hardcoded personal file paths, thus unable to determine whether it needs updating. Examined it's code, found its `for-in` and `while` handler actually assumes the new loop usage. This is confusing since the loop was originally not used like so. Anyway it doesn't need changing (from personal view).

* fix(determineNextNode): fix loop siblings not executed

- remove dead code that prevents any node being the sibling of a loop controller from execution
- enhance traverser loop nodes detection and improve safety

* chore(lint): lint code

This can ensure a loop with >= 3 nodes can run and make execution more stable (`any` can break in-loop logic).
- use more elegant and intuitive method to define loops: set loop start and end, wire edges **inside the loop**, then wire incoming and breaking nodes directly to the loop instead of nodes inside the loop. This reduces rewiring overhead and is the similar pattern used by popular workflow products.

fix: FlowRuntime constructor registry handling for DI containers

The container-based FlowRuntime configuration was failing because the
node registry from createDefaultContainer was an object, but FlowRuntime
expected a Map. Additionally, built-in nodes were missing in the
container path. Fixed by converting the registry object to a Map and
ensuring built-ins are included in both direct and container
initialization paths.

fix: prevent infinite loop with negative concurrency values

Negative concurrency values (like -1) were causing an infinite loop in
the batch execution logic because the for loop i += concurrency would
never progress toward termination when concurrency was
negative. This change clamps invalid concurrency values to fall back to
the default unlimited concurrency, ensuring graceful handling of edge
cases while maintaining backward compatibility.

feat: add workflow versioning for distributed systems

Implement workflow versioning to prevent state corruption during rolling
deployments in distributed environments.

**Key Changes**:

- Blueprint Metadata: Add optional version field to
WorkflowBlueprintMetadata interface
- Version Checking: BaseDistributedAdapter now validates blueprint
versions before processing jobs
- Context Storage: Store blueprint version in distributed context for
compatibility checks
- Error Handling: Reject jobs with version mismatches, allowing
correctly-versioned workers to process them
- Tests: Add comprehensive test coverage for version checking logic
- Documentation: Update API docs and guides to explain versioning
feature

**Benefits**:

- Zero-downtime deployments with safe coexistence of workflow versions
- State corruption prevention in distributed systems
- Backward compatibility with existing unversioned blueprints
- Type safety improvements across the codebase

This feature enables reliable workflow updates in production distributed
systems without service interruption.

feat: add heartbeat mechanism for long-running distributed jobs

- Add extendTTL method to ICoordinationStore interface for extending key
TTLs
- Implement extendTTL in RedisCoordinationStore and
DynamoDbCoordinationStore
- Add periodic TTL extension in BaseDistributedAdapter during job
execution
- Update test mocks and documentation to reflect the new interface
method

This prevents coordination lock expiration in long-running jobs (>1
hour), avoiding double execution race conditions in distributed
workflows. The heartbeat runs every 30 minutes, extending TTLs for join
locks, fan-in counters, and blueprint keys.

feat(flow): automate batch edge wiring for intuitive API

Following the recent DX improvement for `loop` nodes, this commit
applies the same intuitive edge wiring logic to `batch` processing
patterns, creating a consistent and highly declarative API.

Previously, connecting nodes to a batch operation required knowledge of
the internal `_scatter` and `_gather` node suffixes. This exposed
implementation details, made the fluent API less intuitive, and was
inconsistent with the simplified edge definition for loops.

This change introduces logic within the `toBlueprint()` method to
automatically re-wire these conceptual edges. The `Flow` builder now
tracks batch definitions and intelligently redirects edges:

-   Edges where the `target` is the conceptual batch ID are re-wired to
the internal `_scatter` node.
-   Edges where the `source` is the conceptual batch ID are re-wired
from the internal `_gather` node.

This unification significantly improves the developer experience by
abstracting away the underlying mechanics of the scatter-gather pattern,
allowing developers to define workflows in a way that is more aligned
with their conceptual graph.

**Before:**

```typescript
const flow = createFlow('batch-example')
  .node('start', /* ... */)
  .batch('double-items', /* ... */)
  .node('sum-results', /* ... */)
  // Unintuitive: Required knowledge of internal node names
  .edge('start', 'double-items_scatter')
  .edge('double-items_gather', 'sum-results');
```

**After:**

```typescript
const flow = createFlow('batch-example')
  .node('start', /* ... */)
  .batch('double-items', /* ... */)
  .node('sum-results', /* ... */)
  // Intuitive: The framework now handles the internal wiring
automatically
  .edge('start', 'double-items')
  .edge('double-items', 'sum-results');
```

feat(testing): enhance createStepper with prev() and reset() methods

This commit enhances the `createStepper` testing utility by adding
`prev()` and `reset()` methods, improving the workflow debugging and
testing experience.

**New Features:**

-   **`reset()`**: A method to revert the stepper to its original
initial state. This allows for re-running a workflow from the beginning
within the same test without needing to create a new stepper instance.

-   **`prev()`**: A method to step backward through the workflow's
execution history. The stepper now maintains a history of states,
allowing developers to revert to a previous step to inspect the context
and traverser, which is invaluable for debugging complex branching
logic.

fix(runtime): traverser from state calculation

The `GraphTraverser.fromState` static method was not correctly
recalculating the execution frontier when loading from a saved state. It
would restore the set of completed nodes but leave the frontier empty,
causing `hasMoreWork()` to return `false` prematurely.

This has been corrected by adding logic to `fromState` that iterates
through the blueprint nodes to rebuild the frontier based on the
restored set of completed nodes and their join strategies.

feat(flow): automate loop exit edge wiring for intuitive API

Previously, defining the exit path from a loop construct was
unintuitive. Users had to know the internal naming convention for the
loop controller (`<loopId>-loop`) and create an explicit edge from it to
the next node in the workflow. This exposed implementation details and
made the fluent API less declarative.

This commit introduces a significant improvement by automating this
wiring within the `toBlueprint()` method. The framework now
intelligently detects when an edge is defined from the `endNodeId` of a
loop to a node outside the loop. It automatically transforms this
intuitive, user-defined edge into a "break" edge originating from the
internal loop controller.

The `loop-controller`'s internal implementation has been updated to emit
`{ action: 'break' }` when the loop condition is no longer met, which
correctly triggers this newly re-wired edge.

This change results in a much cleaner and more intuitive developer
experience, allowing users to define workflows in a more natural, visual
way without needing to understand the underlying mechanics of the loop
controller.

**Before:**

```typescript
const flow = createFlow('loop-example')
  // ... node definitions
  .loop('counter', {
    startNodeId: 'increment',
    endNodeId: 'increment',
    condition: 'count < 5',
  })
  .edge('initialize', 'increment')
  // Unintuitive: Required for the workflow to proceed after the loop
  .edge('counter-loop', 'finalize', { action: 'break' });
```

**After:**

```typescript
const flow = createFlow('loop-example')
  // ... node definitions
  .loop('counter', {
    startNodeId: 'increment',
    endNodeId: 'increment',
    condition: 'count < 5',
  })
  .edge('initialize', 'increment')
  // Intuitive: The framework now handles the internal wiring
automatically
  .edge('increment', 'finalize');
```

The test suite has also been updated to reflect this new, more intuitive
API for defining loop exits.
lint

fix(runtime): make fallbacks implicitly inherit successors

Previously, a workflow would deadlock if a node with a configured
fallback failed. The root cause was that downstream nodes were still
waiting for the original failed node to complete, which would never
happen. The fallback node's success did not satisfy the predecessor
requirement for the original node's successors.

This forced users to define an explicit, unintuitive edge from the
fallback node to the failed node's children, which is poor DX and breaks
the "substitute" paradigm of a fallback.

This change corrects the logic in `WorkflowLogicHandler`. When
`determineNextNodes` is called, it now checks if the completed node was
acting as a fallback for another node. If so, it "inherits" the outgoing
edges from the original failed node, allowing the workflow to continue
seamlessly down the intended path.

This resolves the deadlock and ensures fallbacks function as a true
substitute in the workflow graph, without requiring any changes to the
user's blueprint definition.

fix(runtime): resolve incorrect 'stalled' status for conditional workflows

Previously, the workflow completion status was determined by comparing
the number of executed nodes to the total number of nodes in the
blueprint. This logic was flawed, as it did not account for valid
workflows with conditional branches where some nodes are intentionally
and correctly skipped.

As a result, a workflow that successfully completed by following a
single path in a conditional graph was incorrectly marked as `stalled`
instead of `completed`.

This commit refactors the status determination logic. The
`WorkflowState.getStatus` method now accepts an `isTraversalComplete`
boolean parameter. The orchestrator, which has visibility into the
execution progress, passes the result of `!traverser.hasMoreWork()` to
this method. This ensures that a workflow is only considered `completed`
when all possible execution paths have been exhausted, leading to an
accurate final status.

fix(runtime): correct subflow resume logic and data flow

This commit addresses a critical bug in the workflow resumption logic,
specifically for workflows awaiting a `SubflowNode`, and fixes
regressions that were discovered during the fix.

The primary issue was that the `runtime.resume` method did not correctly
extract the output from a completed subflow. It passed the entire
subflow context as the node's output, leading to data corruption and
failures in downstream nodes.

Key changes:

- Consistent Subflow Output on Resume: The `resume` method in
`FlowRuntime` now mirrors the output-extraction logic from
`SubflowNode.exec`. It correctly identifies the subflow's terminal
node(s) to produce the correct output shape, ensuring data consistency
between initial runs and resumed runs.

- Correct Fan-in Behavior After Resume: A regression was fixed where
fan-in nodes would behave incorrectly after a resume. The `resume`
method now calculates and passes the `allPredecessors` map to the
`applyEdgeTransform` function. This preserves the rule that fan-in nodes
only receive input when explicitly mapped, making resume behavior
consistent with normal execution.

- SubflowNode Return Type: Corrected the `SubflowNode` to always return
a `{ output: ... }` object, adhering to the `NodeResult` contract and
preventing potential type errors.

- Orchestrator Robustness: The orchestrator now creates a new
`ExecutionContext` with the latest dynamic blueprint before executing a
batch of nodes. This ensures that dynamically generated nodes (e.g., in
a `batch` operation) are correctly found by the executor.

refactor(runtime): extract specialized logic handlers from FlowRuntime

Decompose the monolithic `FlowRuntime` class by extracting specific
responsibilities into dedicated helper classes:
- Create `NodeExecutorFactory` to handle the instantiation of node
executors and resolution of execution strategies.
- Create `WorkflowLogicHandler` to encapsulate core graph traversal
logic, including `determineNextNodes`, `applyEdgeTransform`, and
`resolveNodeInput`.
- Update `FlowRuntime` to delegate these tasks to the new handlers.
- Simplify `FlowRuntime.run` and `resume` methods by extracting
execution context setup into private helper methods.

refactor(runtime): decouple core components and unify execution state

This major refactor overhauls the core workflow execution engine to
improve modularity, simplify state management, and enhance robustness,
particularly for complex scenarios like nested subflows.

The primary motivations for this change were:
- Tight coupling between `FlowRuntime`, `Orchestrator`, and
`NodeExecutor`.
- "Parameter drilling," where numerous arguments (`logger`, `signal`,
`functionRegistry`, etc.) were passed through multiple layers, leading
to brittle code and bugs.
- A dual-registry system for node implementations (a global object and a
dynamic map) which caused issues in subflow execution, as the dynamic
registry was not correctly propagated.
- Hardcoded logic for built-in nodes (`wait`, `subflow`, `batch-*`) in a
large `switch` statement, violating the Open/Closed Principle.

The refactor implements three core changes:

1.  **Introduced `ExecutionContext`:**
    - A new class that encapsulates all state and dependencies for a
single workflow run (`blueprint`, `state`, `nodeRegistry`, `services`,
`signal`, etc.).
    - This object is created once per `run()` and passed down the
execution stack, eliminating parameter drilling and making dependency
management explicit.
    - Includes a `createForSubflow` factory to ensure reliable
inheritance of dependencies.

2.  **Unified Node Registries:**
    - At the start of each `run()`, the global and dynamic (from
`createFlow`) node registries are merged into a single, definitive
`Map`.
    - This map is stored in the `ExecutionContext` and serves as the
single source of truth for node lookups, resolving the subflow registry
bug.

3.  **Promoted Built-in Nodes to First-Class:**
    - Re-implemented all built-in nodes (`subflow`, `wait`,
`batch-scatter`, `batch-gather`) as standard classes extending
`BaseNode`.
    - These classes are now registered by default in the `FlowRuntime`
constructor and behave identically to user-defined nodes.
    - This required enhancing `NodeContext` to provide secure access to
core dependencies like `runtime` and `workflowState`.
    - As a result, the large `_executeBuiltInNode` method and
`BuiltInNodeExecutor` class were entirely removed, significantly
simplifying the `FlowRuntime`.

This refactor makes the codebase easier to reason about, maintain, and
extend. It directly resolves the underlying issues that prevented the
nested HITL subflow test from passing.

docs: orchestrators guide semantics

feat(core): introduce dependency injection container

This commit introduces a lightweight Dependency Injection (DI) container
to the core of the framework, implementing the Inversion of Control
(IoC) principle. This is a major architectural enhancement designed to
increase flexibility, testability, and extensibility without introducing
breaking changes.

**Motivation**

Previously, core components like `FlowRuntime` were tightly coupled to
their dependencies (e.g., `NullLogger`, `JsonSerializer`). They
instantiated default implementations directly, making it difficult to:
- Swap out services without modifying the core.
- Mock dependencies effectively in tests.
- Centrally manage the framework's configuration.

feat: add batch:start event emission and toGraphRepresentation method

- Add batch:start event type to FlowcraftEvent union in types.ts
- Emit batch:start event in runtime.ts during batch-scatter execution
- Add comprehensive tests for toGraphRepresentation method in
flow.test.ts
- Document toGraphRepresentation method in API docs (flow.md)
- Update visualizing-workflows guide to mention toGraphRepresentation
for UI visualization

refactor(runtime): extract execution loop into a pluggable orchestrator

The core workflow execution logic (the `while` loop in
`orchestrateExecution`) has been moved from `FlowRuntime` into a new
`RunToCompletionOrchestrator` class.

This change is driven by the new `IOrchestrator` interface, which
decouples the execution strategy from the runtime's setup
responsibilities. `FlowRuntime` now acts as a facade, preparing
dependencies and delegating the execution process.

This new architecture makes the system more extensible, allowing for
future orchestrators like a step-by-step debugger or specialized
orchestrators for distributed adapters.

readme_html:
flowcraft

Build complex, multi-step processes with a lightweight, composable, and type-safe approach. Model complex business processes, data pipelines, ETL workflows, or AI agents and scale from in-memory scripts to distributed systems without changing the core business logic.

Key Features

  • Zero Dependencies: Lightweight and dependency-free, ensuring easy integration in any runtime.
  • Declarative Workflows: Define workflows as serializable objects with nodes and edges.
  • Unopinionated Logic: Nodes can be simple functions or structured classes, supporting any logic.
  • Progressive Scalability: Run in-memory or scale to distributed systems using the same blueprint.
  • Resilient Execution: Built-in support for retries, fallbacks, timeouts, and cancellation.
  • Advanced Patterns: Includes batches, loops, subflows, and HITL constructs for complex workflows.
  • Extensibility: Pluggable loggers, evaluators, serializers, and middleware for custom behavior.
  • Static Analysis: Tools to detect cycles, validate blueprints, and generate visual diagrams.
  • Type-Safe API: Fully typed with TypeScript for a robust developer experience.

Installation

npm install flowcraft

Usage

Define and run a simple workflow in a few lines of code.

import { createFlow, FlowRuntime } from 'flowcraft'

// 1. Define your functions for the nodes
async function startNode({ context }: NodeContext) {
	const output = await context.get('value')
	return { output }
}
async function doubleNode({ input }: NodeContext) {
	return { output: input * 2 }
}

// 2. Define the workflow structure
const flow = createFlow('simple-workflow')
	.node('start', startNode)
	.node('double', doubleNode)
	.edge('start', 'double')

// 3. Initialize the runtime
const runtime = new FlowRuntime()

// 4. Execute the workflow
async function run() {
	const result = await runtime.run(blueprint, { value: 42 })
	console.log(result.context) // { start: 42, double: 84 }
	console.log(result.status) // 'completed'
}

run()

Core Concepts

  • Blueprint: A serializable object that represents the structure of your workflow. It contains all the nodes and edges and can be stored as JSON or YAML. This is the single source of truth for a workflow's logic.
  • Node: A single unit of work. Node logic can be implemented as a simple async function or a structured class that extends BaseNode for more complex lifecycle management.
  • Edge: A connection between two nodes that defines the direction of the flow. Edges can be conditional, allowing you to create branching logic based on the output or action of a source node.
  • Runtime: The FlowRuntime is the engine that interprets a blueprint and executes its nodes in the correct order. It manages state, handles resiliency, and coordinates the entire process.
  • Context: An object that holds the state of a single workflow execution. The outputs of completed nodes are stored in the context and can be accessed by subsequent nodes.

Resiliency and Error Handling

Design robust workflows with built-in resiliency features.

  • Retries: Configure the maxRetries property on a node to automatically retry it on failure.
  • Fallbacks: Specify a fallback node ID in a node's configuration. If the node fails all its retry attempts, the fallback node will be executed instead, preventing the entire workflow from failing.

For more granular control, you can implement a node using the BaseNode class, which provides prep, exec, post, fallback, and recover lifecycle methods.

Tooling and Utilities

Flowcraft includes tools to help you validate and visualize your workflows.

  • Linter (lintBlueprint): Statically analyze a blueprint to find common errors, such as orphan nodes, invalid edges, or nodes with missing implementations.
  • Analysis (analyzeBlueprint): Programmatically inspect a blueprint to detect cycles, find start/terminal nodes, and get other graph metrics.
  • Diagram Generation (generateMermaid): Automatically generate a Mermaid syntax string from a blueprint to easily visualize your workflow's structure.

Extensibility and Customization

The FlowRuntime can be configured with pluggable components to tailor its behavior to your specific needs:

  • Logger: Provide a custom ILogger implementation (e.g., Pino, Winston) to integrate with your existing logging infrastructure.
  • Serializer: Replace the default JsonSerializer with a more robust one (e.g., superjson) to handle complex data types like Date, Map, and Set in the workflow context.
  • Evaluator: Swap the default PropertyEvaluator for a more powerful expression engine (like jsep or govaluate) to enable complex logic in edge conditions. For trusted environments, an UnsafeEvaluator is also available.
  • Middleware: Wrap node execution with custom logic for cross-cutting concerns like distributed tracing, performance monitoring, or advanced authorization.
  • Event Bus: An event emitter for monitoring workflow and node lifecycle events (workflow:start, node:finish, etc.).

Distributed Execution

Flowcraft's architecture is designed for progressive scalability. The BaseDistributedAdapter provides a foundation for running workflows across multiple machines. Flowcraft provides official adapters for BullMQ, AWS, GCP, Azure, RabbitMQ, and Kafka.
